heroOverview: Actinomycetoma is a chronic granulomatous infection of the subcutaneous tissue, deep fascia, and bone caused by filamentous bacteria — principally Nocardia species and Actinomadura madurae — rather than true fungi. It accounts for roughly 60% of all mycetoma cases globally, with the highest burden in the so-called 'mycetoma belt' stretching across the Sahel, Indian subcontinent, and parts of Latin America. The hallmark triad is a painless subcutaneous swelling, formation of interconnected sinus tracts, and production of visible granules discharged through the skin surface. Without treatment, the infection progressively destroys muscle and bone, typically in the foot or hand, often requiring amputation. Early diagnosis and prolonged combination antibiotic therapy can achieve cure or lasting remission in the majority of patients.

keyStats.prevalence: Exact global prevalence is unknown; WHO estimates tens of thousands of active cases in endemic regions, with Sudan, Mexico, and India reporting the highest case counts
keyStats.demographics: Peak incidence in adults aged 20-40; male-to-female ratio approximately 3-4:1 in most series, reflecting occupational soil exposure
keyStats.avgAge: Most commonly diagnosed in adults aged 20-40 who work in agriculture or walk barefoot
keyStats.globalCases: Over 8,700 cases reported from Sudan alone in one national registry; true global burden substantially underreported

definition: Actinomycetoma (ICD-10: B47.1) is a chronic, progressively destructive, subcutaneous infection caused by filamentous actinobacteria, predominantly Nocardia brasiliensis, Nocardia asteroides complex, Actinomadura madurae, Actinomadura pelletieri, and Streptomyces somaliensis. It represents the bacterial subtype of mycetoma, distinguishing it from eumycetoma, which is caused by true fungi. The causative organisms are soil-dwelling saprophytes introduced into the body by a thorn prick, splinter, or minor traumatic inoculation. Once inoculated, the bacteria form aggregated colonies — termed grains or granules — that elicit a host granulomatous and suppurative response. The grains are visible to the naked eye and vary in colour and texture according to the species: white-to-yellow for Nocardia, red for A. pelletieri, and white-to-pale yellow for A. madurae. The infection spreads along fascial planes, invading deep tissues and bone without crossing intact joints initially. It falls under the specialty of infectious disease, sometimes in collaboration with dermatology and orthopaedic surgery. Actinomycetoma is classified by the WHO as a neglected tropical disease, reflecting its disproportionate impact on impoverished rural populations with limited health access.

typesClassification.description: Caused by Nocardia brasiliensis (most common in Latin America and parts of Africa) or Nocardia asteroides complex; grains are typically soft and white to yellow; usually amenable to cotrimoxazole-based regimens.
The most prevalent form in Africa and India; produces large white to pale-yellow grains; generally requires longer treatment courses and may be less responsive than Nocardia species.
Produces distinctive red grains; found mainly in West Africa; often more resistant and associated with greater destructive potential.
Produces yellow to brown grains; prevalent in the dry Sahel zone; treatment response is variable.

primarySymptoms: A firm, painless or mildly tender subcutaneous swelling, most commonly over the foot (Madura foot) or hand, that grows slowly over months to years.
Multiple discharging sinus tracts opening onto the overlying skin, releasing seropurulent or haemoserous fluid that typically contains visible coloured granules.
Progressive localised swelling that deforms the contour of the affected limb, eventually producing a 'tumorous' appearance.
Visible or palpable granules in the discharge — white, yellow, or red depending on the causative species — which are pathognomonic when present.
Dull, aching deep pain in later stages as infection invades bone and periosteum, sometimes without disproportionate surface inflammation.
Overlying skin changes including hyperpigmentation, induration, and loss of normal tissue planes as fibrosis and suppuration advance.
Restricted range of movement of the foot, ankle, or hand when periarticular structures are involved, though joint space is relatively preserved early.
Regional lymphadenopathy in a minority of patients, more common with Nocardia species.
Systemic symptoms such as fever and weight loss are uncommon in uncomplicated cases but may appear with secondary bacterial superinfection or widespread disease.
Bone destruction evident as pathological fracture, bony deformity, or loss of cortical integrity on imaging in advanced cases.
earlyWarningSigns: A small, painless firm nodule appearing weeks to months after a penetrating thorn or splinter injury in an endemic area.
Very slow local swelling that does not resolve as a simple abscess would.
First appearance of a pinhead-sized draining sinus with coloured granules in the discharge.
Gradual induration of the skin overlying the original wound site without marked redness or heat.
emergencySigns: Sudden marked increase in swelling with systemic fever suggesting secondary bacterial superinfection requiring urgent parenteral antibiotics.
Pathological fracture of an affected bone requiring orthopaedic stabilisation.
Signs of compartment syndrome — tense swelling, severe pain, pallor, pulselessness — in a rapidly expanding lesion.
Spread of sinus tracts to involve the dorsum and plantar surface simultaneously with bone destruction, indicating imminent need for surgical evaluation.

causes.description: The most frequent actinomycetoma pathogen in Mexico and Latin America; introduced through thorn pricks or soil-contaminated minor wounds; invades subcutaneous tissue and forms aggregated bacterial colonies that elicit granulomatous inflammation and sinus tract formation.

A ubiquitous soil actinobacterium in sub-Saharan Africa and South Asia; after traumatic implantation it multiplies within host tissues, forming white-yellow grains, and spreads along fascial planes with progressive destruction of muscle and cortical bone.

Produces uniquely red grains after inoculation; predominantly found in West African soil; elicits an intense suppurative and fibrotic response, often making the lesion harder and more destructive than other actinomycetoma species.

Prevalent in dry Sahel and Horn of Africa soils; produces hard yellow-brown grains; the infection is slow-growing but can achieve large volume and extensive bone involvement before diagnosis, partly because it causes less acute inflammation.

The single most consistent exposure route; agricultural workers and children who walk barefoot sustain repeated micro-injuries from thorns, stones, and plant material, providing entry portals for soil-dwelling actinobacteria in endemic zones.

Farmers, herders, and forestry workers in the 'mycetoma belt' face daily exposure to Nocardia-laden soil; disrupted skin from blisters, cuts, or insect bites amplifies the inoculation risk, explaining the male predominance and working-age peak.

riskFactors.factor: Living or working in endemic regions
riskFactors.description: Residence in the mycetoma belt — Sudan, Niger, Chad, Ethiopia, Mexico, Venezuela, India, Senegal — places individuals in prolonged contact with soil harbouring Nocardia and Actinomadura species; more than 70% of cases originate in these areas.
Farming, herding, and forestry involve daily soil contact and repeated minor trauma; wearing protective footwear and gloves materially reduces inoculation risk.
Barefoot walking accounts for the foot predominance of mycetoma; the plantar surface sustains frequent micro-trauma from thorns, and shoes virtually eliminate this exposure.

Men constitute 70-80% of reported actinomycetoma cases, largely attributed to occupational patterns rather than inherent biological susceptibility; the disparity narrows in communities where women also work in agriculture.
Peak incidence falls in economically active adults during their most productive working years, reflecting maximum soil exposure; cases in children and older adults exist but are less frequent.

Diagnostic and treatment delays of 5-10 years are common in endemic regions; the longer the interval, the greater the bone involvement and the lower the cure rate, making healthcare proximity a strong prognostic factor.

Protein-calorie malnutrition impairs the granulomatous response that limits spread; HIV co-infection appears to accelerate progression in case series from sub-Saharan Africa, though the causal relationship needs more study.

affectedDemographics: Agricultural workers aged 20-40 in the Sahel, Indian subcontinent, and Latin America
Men more than women (roughly 3:1 ratio), reflecting occupational soil exposure
Rural subsistence farmers and herders who walk barefoot
Populations in Sudan, Mexico, India, Nigeria, Senegal, and Niger, which contribute the majority of documented cases
Children and adolescents in highly endemic regions who participate in field work
Immigrants from endemic regions presenting to clinicians in Europe, North America, or Australia who are unfamiliar with the diagnosis
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis rests on recognising the clinical triad of swelling, sinus tracts, and granule discharge in a patient from an endemic region. The first diagnostic priority is to identify and characterise the granules, because their colour, size, and texture guide both species identification and treatment choice. Direct microscopy of crushed granules in 10-20% KOH mounts shows the thin filamentous structure of actinobacteria (1 µm in diameter), distinguishing actinomycetoma from eumycetoma, whose fungi produce broader hyphae (2-6 µm). Gram staining and modified Ziehl-Neelsen staining of granules help narrow the genus: Nocardia are weakly acid-fast, Actinomadura are not. Culture of granules on brain-heart infusion agar and Sabouraud dextrose agar (to exclude fungal growth) at 37°C for up to four weeks identifies the species. PCR-based sequencing of the 16S rRNA gene now provides definitive species identification and is recommended whenever available, given its direct impact on treatment choice. Plain radiographs of the affected foot or hand reveal periosteal reaction, cortical erosion, the pathognomonic 'dot-in-circle' sign on MRI, and cavitary bone lesions. MRI is the most sensitive imaging modality, demonstrating grain-containing cavities within the muscle and the extent of bony and soft-tissue involvement, guiding surgical planning. The key differential is eumycetoma (B47.0), which is caused by true fungi such as Madurella mycetomatis and requires antifungal rather than antibiotic therapy — making correct species classification clinically critical.

diagnosticTests.test: Granule microscopy (KOH mount and Gram stain)
diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line rapid test to confirm mycetoma type; thin filaments (1 µm) indicate actinomycetoma; distinguishes from eumycetoma fungal hyphae (2-6 µm) and guides immediate treatment planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small amount of discharge containing granules is collected from an active sinus tract, placed on a glass slide, and examined under the microscope within minutes in the clinic or laboratory.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative actinobacterial species, critical because Nocardia-related actinomycetoma responds better to cotrimoxazole than Actinomadura or Streptomyces species, which may need amikacin-based regimens.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Granules are inoculated on brain-heart infusion agar and Sabouraud dextrose agar and incubated at 37°C; results take 2-4 weeks because these organisms grow slowly.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Definitive species identification with higher sensitivity than culture, especially for organisms that grow poorly on standard media; directly informs the antibiotic regimen.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A tissue biopsy or granule sample is sent to a reference or research laboratory; turnaround time is typically 1-2 weeks.

diagnosticTests.test: Plain radiograph of the affected extremity
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects periosteal reaction, cortical erosion, cavitary bone lesions, and pathological fracture; used to stage extent of bony involvement and monitor response to treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard X-ray of the foot or hand, taking a few minutes; multiple views are taken; findings may be subtle in early disease.

diagnosticTests.purpose: The most sensitive modality for characterising soft-tissue and bone involvement; the 'dot-in-circle' sign on T2 sequences represents grain-containing cavities and is highly specific for mycetoma; guides surgical planning.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A 30-60 minute scan in a magnetic resonance scanner, usually without contrast for initial staging; may require injection of gadolinium contrast for detailed anatomical delineation.

diagnosticTests.test: Fine-needle aspiration or punch biopsy with histopathology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides tissue for granule characterisation, culture, and PCR when discharge is insufficient; confirms granulomatous reaction pattern and excludes neoplasm.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A narrow needle or punch device samples the deepest palpable tissue under local anaesthetic; sent for both microbiology and histopathology processing.

treatmentOverview: Actinomycetoma is treated with prolonged combination antibiotic regimens tailored to the causative species. Unlike eumycetoma, it does not respond to antifungals — a fact that makes precise species identification the critical first step before prescribing. The cornerstone of treatment for Nocardia-related actinomycetoma is high-dose cotrimoxazole (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole), often combined with amikacin given in 3-week induction cycles for moderate-to-severe or bone-involving disease. For Actinomadura madurae and Actinomadura pelletieri, combination regimens incorporating cotrimoxazole, dapsone, rifampicin, and amikacin are standard, guided by WHO treatment protocols and published series from the Mycetoma Research Centre in Sudan. Streptomyces somaliensis responds most consistently to streptomycin-cotrimoxazole combinations. Treatment duration is prolonged — typically 12-24 months for soft-tissue disease and up to 36 months or longer for bone-invasive disease — because the dense granule architecture limits antibiotic penetration into the bacterial colonies. Response is monitored with serial clinical examination, plain radiographs every 3-6 months, and MRI to assess granuloma resolution. Surgery plays an adjunctive role: wide local excision of localised early lesions can improve cure rates when combined with perioperative antibiotics; amputation is reserved for end-stage disease with complete bone destruction, uncontrolled secondary infection, or failure of multiple medical regimens. Early diagnosis and uninterrupted antibiotic treatment provide the best chance of limb salvage and durable cure.

medicalTreatments.name: Cotrimoxazole (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole)
medicalTreatments.description: The foundational antibiotic for actinomycetoma; given at high doses (trimethoprim 20 mg/kg/day in 2-3 divided doses) continuously for 12-24 months or longer; active against Nocardia species and provides meaningful activity against Actinomadura in combination regimens.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates of 60-90% for Nocardia actinomycetoma with prolonged courses; lower (30-50%) for Actinomadura species when used alone, underscoring the need for combination therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Amikacin (induction phase)
medicalTreatments.description: An aminoglycoside given intravenously or intramuscularly in 3-week cycles (usually 3 cycles on, 3 weeks off) alongside cotrimoxazole for moderate-to-severe or bone-involving disease; provides bactericidal activity against Nocardia and Actinomadura species where cotrimoxazole alone is insufficient.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Combined cotrimoxazole-amikacin achieves clinical improvement or cure in approximately 70-80% of moderate actinomycetoma cases in published series from Sudan and Mexico.

medicalTreatments.name: Dapsone with cotrimoxazole
medicalTreatments.description: An older combination still used in resource-limited settings for Actinomadura madurae disease; dapsone's anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties complement sulfonamide activity; given long-term at 1.5 mg/kg/day alongside cotrimoxazole.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical cure or significant improvement reported in 50-70% of A. madurae cases in retrospective series, though amikacin-based regimens are now preferred where available.

medicalTreatments.name: Rifampicin with cotrimoxazole
medicalTreatments.description: Rifampicin 600 mg daily is added to cotrimoxazole for Streptomyces somaliensis actinomycetoma and for cases failing first-line cotrimoxazole alone; its tissue-penetrating ability is valuable in fibrosed granulomatous lesions.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Response rates for S. somaliensis of approximately 60-75% with combination therapy in case series from Sudan and the Horn of Africa.

medicalTreatments.name: Linezolid
medicalTreatments.description: An oxazolidinone with excellent activity against Nocardia and Actinomadura species; reserved for refractory or multidrug-resistant actinomycetoma where standard regimens have failed; used for 6-12 months with monitoring for haematological toxicity.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Case series report microbiological cure or substantial improvement in more than 70% of refractory Nocardia actinomycetoma with linezolid-containing salvage regimens.

medicalTreatments.name: Imipenem or meropenem (severe refractory disease)
medicalTreatments.description: Carbapenems provide broad-spectrum bactericidal activity against resistant Nocardia species; typically given for 3-week parenteral induction cycles in patients with rapidly progressive or extensively bone-invasive disease.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Clinical response documented in 60-80% of carbapenem-treated Nocardia actinomycetoma cases in specialist centre series, most used in combination with cotrimoxazole or linezolid.

surgicalOptions.name: Wide local excision
surgicalOptions.description: Surgical removal of localised actinomycetoma lesions with a margin of healthy tissue; most effective in small, well-demarcated, superficial lesions without bone involvement; best combined with antibiotic therapy before and after surgery to reduce recurrence.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Recurrence rates of 20-30% after excision alone; combined with antibiotics, long-term disease control is achieved in approximately 60-70% of carefully selected early cases.

surgicalOptions.name: Amputation
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for end-stage actinomycetoma with complete destruction of the bony architecture of the foot or hand, uncontrolled secondary sepsis, or disease that has progressed through multiple antibiotic regimens; a last resort to save the patient's life or permit ambulation with a prosthesis.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Curative for the infected extremity; 10-15% recurrence at the stump margin in incomplete resections; outcome is otherwise favourable with proper postoperative antibiotic cover.

alternativeTreatments.name: Protective footwear as primary prevention
alternativeTreatments.description: Wearing shoes or sandals in endemic areas dramatically reduces inoculation; community programmes distributing footwear in the mycetoma belt have been associated with lower incidence in observational data.

alternativeTreatments.name: Traditional wound treatment
alternativeTreatments.description: Some communities apply topical plant extracts to draining lesions; none have proven microbiological efficacy, and they may delay presentation; clinicians in endemic regions often encounter patients after years of such treatment.

whySeeSpecialist: Actinomycetoma requires specialist management because accurate species identification changes the antibiotic regimen, treatment duration extends to 1-3 years, and monitoring for bone destruction determines when surgery is needed. An infectious disease physician with experience in neglected tropical diseases, ideally working with a dermatologist and orthopaedic surgeon, provides the multi-disciplinary care that gives the best chance of limb salvage. Patients should not attempt to manage this condition in primary care without specialist input; early specialist referral is the single most modifiable factor in avoiding amputation.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an infectious disease specialist or tropical medicine physician at a centre with experience in mycetoma or neglected tropical diseases. Ideally, the centre should have on-site capacity for granule culture, PCR species identification, and MRI staging. In non-endemic countries, academic medical centres with tropical medicine units or mycology reference laboratories are the appropriate referral destination. Ask whether the clinician has access to WHO mycetoma protocols and can prescribe prolonged amikacin cycles with therapeutic drug monitoring.
preventionStrategies: Wear shoes, sandals, or protective boots when walking outdoors in endemic regions; footwear is the single most effective preventive measure and reduces inoculation risk to near zero for the foot.
Wear gloves during agricultural work involving soil, especially when handling thorny plants or digging.
Clean any penetrating wound from a thorn or splinter promptly with soap and water; seek early medical review if swelling develops over subsequent weeks.
Community-level neglected tropical disease programmes providing footwear and health education have shown measurable impact in reduction of new cases in Sudan and Mexico.
Early presentation at the first sign of a persisting nodule or sinus tract prevents bone involvement and dramatically improves the chance of medical cure without surgery.
lifestyleModifications: Complete all prescribed antibiotic courses in full — treatment lasts 1-3 years and must not be stopped when the wound appears to close.
Attend all follow-up appointments for serial radiographs and clinical assessment; monitoring determines when treatment can be safely stopped.
Protect the affected limb from secondary trauma; use crutches or appropriate footwear to offload the foot if advised by the treating team.
Maintain good nutrition: adequate protein and caloric intake supports the immune granulomatous response that helps contain infection.
Limit alcohol during amikacin therapy; stay well hydrated to protect kidney function during aminoglycoside cycles.
Report any new nodules or draining sinuses promptly, as secondary Nocardia lesions can occasionally appear at remote sites.

recommended: High-protein diet (meat, legumes, eggs) to support immune function and tissue repair during prolonged treatment
Adequate caloric intake to prevent the malnutrition that is common in affected populations and impairs host defences
Plenty of water, especially during amikacin cycles, to maintain renal clearance of the drug
Vitamin- and mineral-rich foods (fruits, vegetables) to support wound healing and immune response
avoid: Alcohol during aminoglycoside therapy, which may potentiate kidney toxicity
Severely calorie-restricted diets that compound the already common problem of malnutrition in endemic populations
Foods that interact with rifampicin or dapsone (specific interactions are medication-specific; discuss with the prescribing clinician)

exerciseGuidelines: Weight-bearing on an affected foot should be minimised during acute phases, particularly when bone involvement is confirmed on imaging. Crutch-assisted mobilisation reduces mechanical stress on granulomatous bone and lowers the risk of pathological fracture. Upper limb lesions do not generally restrict ambulation. Gentle range-of-motion exercises of unaffected joints prevent deconditioning during the prolonged treatment period. Full return to agricultural work should await confirmation of clinical and radiological response.
dailyManagement: Take antibiotics at the same time each day with food to improve tolerability and maintain consistent blood levels.
Keep a treatment diary recording antibiotic doses, any new symptoms (hearing changes during amikacin, peripheral tingling from cotrimoxazole), and wound status.
Change wound dressings daily on any open sinus tracts using clean technique to prevent secondary bacterial contamination.
Monitor for cotrimoxazole side effects including rash, bone marrow suppression (check CBC every 2-3 months), and elevated creatinine.
During amikacin cycles, attend audiometry and creatinine monitoring as scheduled; report any hearing loss or decreased urine output immediately.
prognosis: Prognosis depends critically on the causative species, extent of bone involvement at diagnosis, and treatment adherence. Nocardia actinomycetoma detected before bone invasion carries cure rates of 60-90% with prolonged cotrimoxazole-based regimens. Actinomadura madurae and A. pelletieri are harder to eradicate: cure in soft-tissue-only disease reaches 50-70% with combination regimens, dropping substantially once bone is invaded. Treatment-naïve patients presenting within the first 1-2 years of symptoms have markedly better outcomes than those presenting after 5 years or more with extensive bone destruction. With optimised specialist management, limb salvage exceeds 70% even in moderate bone-involving disease. Without treatment or with incomplete courses, infection progresses inexorably, leading to complete destruction of the foot or hand and eventual septic systemic complications. Long-term follow-up is essential, as recurrence after apparent cure occurs in 10-30% of cases.
recoveryTimeline: Initial clinical improvement — reduction in swelling, decreased discharge, fewer active sinus tracts — typically occurs within 3-6 months of starting effective antibiotic therapy. Radiological improvement on plain X-ray lags clinical response by several months; MRI evidence of healing may not appear for 6-12 months. Total treatment duration is 12-24 months for soft-tissue disease and 24-36 months or longer when bone is involved. After treatment completion, patients are followed annually for at least 3-5 years to detect recurrence. Return to full physical work capacity depends on residual bone and soft-tissue integrity.
complications: Progressive bone destruction — cortical erosion, medullary cavitation, and eventually complete osteolysis of the metatarsals, tarsals, and phalanges, causing severe deformity and loss of function.
Pathological fracture — weakened bone from infection and granulomatous replacement fractures under normal loading, requiring orthopaedic stabilisation and changing the surgical prognosis.

Secondary bacterial superinfection — open sinus tracts allow entry of Staphylococcus aureus, streptococci, and Gram-negative organisms, causing acute flares of cellulitis, abscess, or sepsis requiring prompt parenteral antibiotics.
Lymphoedema — chronic obstruction of lymphatic drainage by fibrosis and recurrent infection causes irreversible limb oedema that persists even after microbiological cure.

Amputation — required in 10-25% of hospitalised patients in large endemic-region series when disease is end-stage; the psychological, occupational, and economic impact is severe in farming communities.

Aminoglycoside toxicity — cumulative amikacin or streptomycin exposure during induction cycles carries a 5-10% risk of permanent sensorineural hearing loss or nephrotoxicity, requiring dose adjustment or drug substitution.

supportResources.name: WHO — Mycetoma (Neglected Tropical Disease)
supportResources.description: WHO factsheet on mycetoma including actinomycetoma, covering epidemiology, diagnosis, treatment, and the NTD programme.

supportResources.name: Mycetoma Research Centre, University of Khartoum
supportResources.description: The world's leading specialist centre for mycetoma research; provides clinical protocols, patient resources, and diagnostic guidance.

supportResources.name: CDC — Nocardiosis
supportResources.description: CDC overview of Nocardia infections including actinomycetoma, with guidance on diagnosis and treatment.

sources.title: Fahal AH. Mycetoma: a thorn in the flesh. Transactions of the Royal Society of Tropical Medicine and Hygiene, 2004
Nenoff P et al. Mycetoma — A Review on the Aetiology, Epidemiology, Diagnosis and Treatment. JDDG, 2021
Ameen M. Mycetoma: rational treatment of infection. Expert Opinion on Pharmacotherapy, 2007
van de Sande WW. Global Burden of Human Mycetoma: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis. PLOS Neglected Tropical Diseases, 2013
CDC — Nocardiosis Information for Healthcare Professionals
Hay RJ et al. Eumycetoma and Actinomycetoma — An Update on Epidemiology, Diagnosis, and Treatment. Current Fungal Infection Reports, 2018

confusedWithConditions.name: Eumycetoma
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Eumycetoma is caused by true fungi (e.g., Madurella mycetomatis) rather than bacteria and requires long-term antifungal therapy with itraconazole rather than antibiotics; granule microscopy showing broad fungal hyphae (2-6 µm) versus thin bacterial filaments (1 µm) is the key distinguishing test.

confusedWithConditions.name: Mycetoma (unspecified)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: The unspecified category is used when the subtype has not been confirmed; species identification is essential because treatment for actinomycetoma (antibiotics) differs completely from that for eumycetoma (antifungals).

confusedWithConditions.name: Cutaneous nocardiosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Cutaneous nocardiosis from direct inoculation produces a lymphocutaneous sporotrichoid pattern or a single abscess without the grain-forming sinus tracts that characterise actinomycetoma; the two conditions overlap in causative organisms but differ in clinical pattern and prognosis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Cutaneous tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tuberculous skin disease may produce chronic ulcerating nodules and regional lymphadenopathy but does not form coloured granule-containing sinus tracts; Ziehl-Neelsen staining and culture for Mycobacterium tuberculosis confirm tuberculosis.

relatedConditions.name: Eumycetoma
relatedConditions.relevance: The fungal subtype of mycetoma; presents identically to actinomycetoma but requires antifungal rather than antibiotic treatment; differentiating the two is the critical first step in management.

relatedConditions.name: Mycetoma (unspecified)
relatedConditions.relevance: The parent diagnostic category encompassing both actinomycetoma and eumycetoma; used when species confirmation is pending.

relatedConditions.name: Lymphocutaneous sporotrichosis
relatedConditions.relevance: A subcutaneous mycosis sharing the same mechanism of traumatic soil/plant inoculation and chronic progressive course; distinguishable by its lymphatic spread pattern and absence of grain formation.

relatedConditions.name: Cutaneous nocardiosis
relatedConditions.relevance: Caused by the same genus of actinobacteria; presents as chronic granulomatous skin infection after inoculation; some Nocardia species cause either cutaneous nocardiosis or actinomycetoma depending on depth of inoculation.

relatedConditions.name: Subcutaneous pheomycotic cyst
relatedConditions.relevance: Another chronic subcutaneous infection from traumatic inoculation of environmental organisms; causes localised nodules and cysts without the grain-forming sinus tracts of mycetoma.

faqs.question: What is actinomycetoma and how is it different from a fungal infection?
faqs.answer: Actinomycetoma is a chronic deep skin and bone infection caused by bacteria called actinobacteria, not fungi. The bacterial type is treated with long-term antibiotics. The fungal type (eumycetoma) requires antifungals instead. A microscope test of the discharge granules tells them apart.

faqs.question: What does actinomycetoma look like?
faqs.answer: It typically appears as a slowly growing painless swelling, most often on the foot, with multiple small tunnel-like openings in the skin that discharge a fluid containing visible coloured grains — white, yellow, or red depending on the bacterium.

faqs.question: How do you catch actinomycetoma?
faqs.answer: It is not contagious. The infection starts when soil bacteria are pushed into the skin through a thorn prick, splinter, or small wound — most often in people who walk barefoot or farm without gloves in tropical or subtropical regions.

faqs.question: Which countries have the most actinomycetoma?
faqs.answer: Sudan, Mexico, India, Nigeria, Senegal, and Chad report the most cases. The disease is concentrated in a region called the mycetoma belt that spans sub-Saharan Africa, the Indian subcontinent, and parts of Latin America.

faqs.question: How long does treatment take?
faqs.answer: Treatment typically lasts 12 to 24 months for infections that have not reached bone, and up to 36 months or longer when bone is involved. The full course must be completed even if the skin appears to heal, because stopping early causes relapse.

faqs.question: What antibiotics are used for actinomycetoma?
faqs.answer: The most common treatment is high-dose cotrimoxazole (trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole) taken daily for 1–3 years. Amikacin injections are added in 3-week cycles for moderate or bone-involving disease. The exact regimen depends on which bacterium is causing the infection.

faqs.question: Can actinomycetoma be cured without surgery?
faqs.answer: Yes, in many cases. Nocardia actinomycetoma detected before bone invasion has cure rates of 60–90% with antibiotics alone. Surgery is considered when disease is localised and caught early, or as a last resort (amputation) when bone is completely destroyed.

faqs.question: Does actinomycetoma always lead to amputation?
faqs.answer: No. Amputation is required in roughly 10–25% of hospitalised patients in large series and is a last resort for end-stage disease. Early diagnosis and sustained antibiotic treatment give most patients a good chance of keeping their limb.

faqs.question: Is actinomycetoma painful?
faqs.answer: In early stages the swelling is often painless, which contributes to delayed diagnosis. As infection invades deeper tissue and bone over months to years, a dull aching pain develops. Sharp severe pain is more associated with secondary bacterial superinfection or pathological fracture.

faqs.question: How is actinomycetoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis starts with examining the granules discharged from sinus tracts under a microscope. Thin filaments (1 µm wide) identify the bacterial type. Culture and DNA testing (16S rRNA sequencing) confirm the specific species. X-ray and MRI assess bone involvement.

faqs.question: Can actinomycetoma spread to other parts of the body?
faqs.answer: It rarely spreads via the bloodstream. It spreads locally along fascial planes to involve deeper muscle and adjacent bones. Regional lymph nodes are occasionally involved. Systemic dissemination is uncommon in people with an otherwise healthy immune system.

faqs.question: Is actinomycetoma the same as Madura foot?
faqs.answer: Madura foot is an older term for mycetoma affecting the foot, including both the bacterial (actinomycetoma) and fungal (eumycetoma) types. When the causative organism is a bacterium such as Nocardia or Actinomadura, it is specifically called actinomycetoma.

faqs.question: Can children get actinomycetoma?
faqs.answer: Yes, though it is most common in adults aged 20–40. Children who participate in agricultural work or walk barefoot in endemic areas are at risk. Cases in adolescents are documented and are managed with the same principles as adult treatment.

faqs.question: Why does actinomycetoma mainly affect the foot?
faqs.answer: The foot is the most common site because the usual route of infection is a thorn prick or small wound sustained while walking barefoot on soil. The same disease can occur on the hand, lower leg, back, or scalp depending on where the inoculation occurs.

faqs.question: What are the coloured grains in the discharge?
faqs.answer: The grains are aggregated bacterial colonies embedded in host tissue. Their colour helps identify the species: white or pale yellow for Nocardia and Actinomadura madurae, red for Actinomadura pelletieri, and yellow-brown for Streptomyces somaliensis. Identifying them guides treatment.

faqs.question: Is actinomycetoma contagious?
faqs.answer: No. It cannot spread from person to person. The infection comes from bacteria that naturally live in soil; it requires a penetrating wound to cause disease. No isolation is needed and normal household contact poses no risk to family members.

faqs.question: How can actinomycetoma be prevented?
faqs.answer: Wearing shoes or sandals is the most effective prevention, as it stops soil bacteria from entering through foot wounds. Gloves protect the hands during agricultural work. Cleaning thorn or splinter wounds promptly and seeking early medical review if a nodule develops prevents progression.

faqs.question: Does HIV infection worsen actinomycetoma?
faqs.answer: HIV co-infection appears to accelerate disease progression and may reduce treatment response based on case series from sub-Saharan Africa, though the relationship is not fully established. People living with HIV in endemic areas should be vigilant for early signs of mycetoma.

faqs.question: What happens if actinomycetoma is left untreated?
faqs.answer: Without treatment the infection slowly but relentlessly destroys deeper soft tissue and bone over years. The foot or hand eventually loses its structural integrity, secondary infections become recurrent, and amputation becomes the only life-saving option.

faqs.question: How do I find a specialist for actinomycetoma outside endemic countries?
faqs.answer: In non-endemic countries such as the United States, UK, or Australia, the best approach is referral to an academic centre with a tropical medicine or infectious disease unit that has access to a mycology or microbiology reference laboratory capable of culture and PCR species identification.

metaDescription: Actinomycetoma causes painless foot swelling, sinus tracts, and granule discharge. Learn diagnosis and antibiotic treatment for this bacterial mycetoma.
